prompt: The email validation regex accepts invalid addresses. What is the bug?
choices: matches() requires anchors ^ and $ to validate the full string
The pattern ".+@.+" requires at least one character before and after @, so "bad@" would return false and "@bad" would also return false
The pattern .+@.+ is too permissive: any characters match '.', so addresses like "a@b" with no TLD or domain are accepted; a stricter pattern is needed for real validation
String.matches cannot be used for email validation; use javax.mail.internet.InternetAddress
explanation: matches() anchors to the full string. ".+@.+" requires 1+ chars, @, then 1+ chars. "bad@" has nothing after @, so .+ fails → returns false. "@bad" has nothing before @, so the first .+ fails → returns false. The pattern is still too permissive for real emails but correctly rejects these two cases. Option b correctly describes what happens.
